history.subject: NestJS Routing, ValidationPipe Options, and Request Lifecycle Components

questions.hint: Think decorators versus imperative route registration.

options.text: Using decorators on controllers and methods for controller-based routing.
options.feedback: Correct: Nest commonly defines routes declaratively with controller and method decorators.

options.text: By calling app.get/app.post directly inside controllers like Express.
options.feedback: Nest does not typically register routes imperatively inside controllers like Express.

options.text: Only through a central routes.json file that Nest reads at runtime.
options.feedback: Nest routing is not driven by a routes.json file by default.

options.text: By generating routes automatically without any controller code.
options.feedback: Nest requires explicit metadata via decorators; it does not auto-generate routes blindly.

questions.question: In NestJS, how are routes typically declared compared to Express?

whyOptions.text: It attaches route metadata to classes and methods for structured routing.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: decorators provide metadata Nest uses to map requests to handlers.

whyOptions.text: It eliminates the need for dependency injection in request handlers.
whyOptions.feedback: DI still exists; decorators mainly define routing and related metadata.

whyOptions.text: It guarantees all routes run without middleware or guards.
whyOptions.feedback: Routes can still use middleware, guards, and interceptors alongside decorators.

whyOptions.text: It forces all validation to run only inside controllers.
whyOptions.feedback: Validation can run globally via pipes, not only inside controllers.

questions.whyQuestion: Why does NestJS favor decorator-based routing on controllers?

options.text: Strips properties that are not in the DTO schema.
options.feedback: Correct: whitelist removes unknown fields so only DTO-declared properties remain.

options.text: Throws a 400 error whenever any extra field is present.
options.feedback: That behavior is tied to forbidNonWhitelisted, not whitelist alone.

options.text: Converts incoming values to match DTO property types.
options.feedback: Type conversion is controlled by transform, not whitelist.

options.text: Automatically adds missing DTO fields with default values.
options.feedback: Whitelist does not add fields; it only removes fields not allowed.

questions.question: What does ValidationPipe option "whitelist: true" do?

whyOptions.text: It prevents unexpected client-supplied fields from reaching business logic.
whyOptions.feedback: Right: stripping unknown input reduces accidental or malicious extra payload usage.

whyOptions.text: It ensures every request is rejected unless all DTO fields are present.
whyOptions.feedback: Whitelist does not enforce required fields; validation decorators do.

whyOptions.text: It guarantees headers and query params are also removed automatically.
whyOptions.feedback: Whitelist targets validated objects, not unrelated headers or query strings.

whyOptions.text: It converts all string inputs into numbers to avoid parsing issues.
whyOptions.feedback: Conversion is handled by transform, not by whitelisting.

questions.whyQuestion: Why might enabling "whitelist: true" improve API safety?

options.text: Returns a 400 error instead of silently stripping unknown fields.
options.feedback: Correct: it rejects payloads containing non-whitelisted properties with a 400.

options.text: Silently removes extra fields and continues processing.
options.feedback: Silent stripping is whitelist behavior; forbidNonWhitelisted rejects the request.

options.text: Allows extra fields but logs a warning for debugging.
options.feedback: This option enforces strictness; it does not merely log warnings.

options.text: Transforms extra fields into DTO fields using type conversion.
options.feedback: Type conversion is unrelated; extra fields are not mapped automatically.

questions.question: What does "forbidNonWhitelisted: true" do when extra fields are sent?

whyOptions.text: It fails fast on unexpected input, making contracts strict and predictable.
whyOptions.feedback: Yes: strict rejection surfaces client mistakes and blocks unapproved payload fields.

whyOptions.text: It automatically authenticates the user before any controller runs.
whyOptions.feedback: Authentication is handled by guards, not by validation pipe settings.

whyOptions.text: It improves performance by skipping DTO validation entirely.
whyOptions.feedback: It does not skip validation; it adds an extra constraint on unknown properties.

whyOptions.text: It ensures middleware can no longer modify the incoming request body.
whyOptions.feedback: Middleware can still run; this only impacts validation outcomes.

questions.whyQuestion: Why would you enable "forbidNonWhitelisted: true" in a public API?

options.text: Converts incoming values to the expected DTO property types when possible.
options.feedback: Correct: transform can coerce types, like turning numeric strings into numbers.

options.text: Removes all properties not explicitly listed in the controller method signature.
options.feedback: Property stripping is handled by whitelist, not by transform.

options.text: Automatically adds missing properties to match the DTO shape.
options.feedback: Transform does not create missing fields; it converts provided values.

options.text: Blocks requests with extra fields by sending a 401 response.
options.feedback: Rejecting extra fields is forbidNonWhitelisted and returns 400, not 401.

questions.question: What does ValidationPipe option "transform: true" primarily enable?

whyOptions.text: It reduces manual parsing by coercing values to declared DTO types.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: it aligns runtime values with DTO types, simplifying validation and logic.

whyOptions.text: It forces all string fields to become numbers automatically.
whyOptions.feedback: Only compatible fields convert; unrelated strings remain strings.

whyOptions.text: It guarantees validation never fails due to incorrect input types.
whyOptions.feedback: Validation can still fail if conversion is impossible or constraints are violated.

whyOptions.text: It ensures guards always run after interceptors for correct types.
whyOptions.feedback: Execution order is separate; transform affects value conversion, not lifecycle ordering.

questions.whyQuestion: Why can "transform: true" be useful when clients send numbers as strings?

options.text: Middleware
options.feedback: Correct: middleware commonly handles cross-cutting preprocessing before routing logic.

options.text: Guard
options.feedback: Guards focus on authorization decisions, not general preprocessing tasks.

options.text: Interceptor
options.feedback: Interceptors wrap controller execution to reshape data, not basic preprocessing.

options.text: Controller decorator
options.feedback: Decorators define metadata; they do not execute preprocessing logic at runtime.

questions.question: Which component is mainly used for preprocessing incoming requests like logging and headers?

whyOptions.text: It can run on broad path patterns and does not decide per-route access.
whyOptions.feedback: Right: middleware often applies across routes for shared request processing concerns.

whyOptions.text: It runs only after the controller has returned a response.
whyOptions.feedback: That behavior matches interceptors; middleware runs before controller execution.

whyOptions.text: It is the only mechanism that can block unauthorized users.
whyOptions.feedback: Guards are designed for authorization; middleware is not the primary auth layer.

whyOptions.text: It can only be attached to one controller method at a time.
whyOptions.feedback: Middleware is often registered broadly, not limited to single methods.

questions.whyQuestion: Why is middleware described as generally route-agnostic?

options.text: Guard
options.feedback: Correct: guards enforce authorization by allowing or blocking access to handlers.

options.text: Middleware
options.feedback: Middleware can inspect requests but is not the standard authorization gate.

options.text: Interceptor
options.feedback: Interceptors transform around execution; they are not meant for access decisions.

options.text: ValidationPipe
options.feedback: Validation checks payload shape and constraints, not identity or roles.

questions.question: Which component decides whether a request is allowed to proceed based on authentication or roles?

whyOptions.text: They enforce access control at specific entry points where permissions vary.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: attaching guards scopes authorization rules to particular controllers or routes.

whyOptions.text: They must run after the response is sent to verify outcomes.
whyOptions.feedback: Guards must run before controllers to prevent unauthorized execution.

whyOptions.text: They are required to strip unknown DTO fields before validation.
whyOptions.feedback: Stripping unknown fields is a validation concern, handled by whitelist options.

whyOptions.text: They replace the router so decorators are no longer needed.
whyOptions.feedback: Decorators still define routing; guards only control whether handlers execute.

questions.whyQuestion: Why are guards typically attached to routes or controllers?

options.text: Interceptor
options.feedback: Correct: interceptors wrap handler execution and can reshape requests and responses.

options.text: Middleware
options.feedback: Middleware runs before routing/handler execution and does not wrap responses similarly.

options.text: Guard
options.feedback: Guards decide access; they do not transform response bodies around execution.

options.text: Module
options.feedback: Modules organize providers and controllers; they are not lifecycle execution hooks.

questions.question: Which component runs before and after the controller and can transform input or output?

whyOptions.text: They can map the handler result before it is sent to the client.
whyOptions.feedback: Yes: interceptors can post-process the returned value to standardize output format.

whyOptions.text: They directly mutate route decorators to change paths at runtime.
whyOptions.feedback: Decorators define metadata at design time; interceptors do not alter routing paths.

whyOptions.text: They are the main mechanism for role-based access control in Nest.
whyOptions.feedback: Role checks belong in guards; interceptors are for transformation and cross-cutting logic.

whyOptions.text: They only execute if validation fails to format errors.
whyOptions.feedback: They can run on successful requests too; error handling is not their only use.

questions.whyQuestion: Why are interceptors suited for reshaping responses?

options.text: Middleware preprocesses requests, while guards decide whether execution is permitted.
options.feedback: Correct: middleware handles preprocessing, while guards enforce authorization decisions.

options.text: Middleware transforms responses, while guards parse request bodies.
options.feedback: Response transformation is interceptor territory; parsing is not a guard’s core job.

options.text: Middleware runs after controllers, while guards run only on errors.
options.feedback: Middleware and guards run before controllers; neither is limited to error cases.

questions.hint: Think of it as overriding the compiler's caution.

options.text: A way to tell the compiler to trust your chosen type despite uncertainty
options.feedback: Correct: it overrides type checking by asserting you know the real type.

options.text: A runtime check that throws if a value is not the asserted type
options.feedback: Type assertions do not add runtime checks; they only affect compile-time types.

options.text: A feature that permanently converts a value into a new runtime type
options.feedback: Assertions do not convert values; they only change the compiler’s view.

options.text: A strict declaration that prevents any later reassignment of the variable
options.feedback: Reassignment rules come from variable declarations, not from assertions.

questions.question: What best describes a TypeScript type assertion?

whyOptions.text: Because it suppresses some safety checks by forcing a specific type view
whyOptions.feedback: Right: you are taking responsibility for correctness instead of the type system.

whyOptions.text: Because it makes TypeScript perform deeper runtime validation automatically
whyOptions.feedback: TypeScript erases types at runtime, so no extra validation occurs.

whyOptions.text: Because it guarantees the asserted type is correct in all code paths
whyOptions.feedback: Assertions can be wrong; they do not prove correctness across paths.

whyOptions.text: Because it prevents any implicit type inference from happening
whyOptions.feedback: Inference still happens elsewhere; assertions only affect the asserted expression.

questions.whyQuestion: Why is a type assertion sometimes described as 'lowering the compiler's guard'?

questions.hint: Focus on delaying the concrete type choice.

options.text: A placeholder type that is decided later or inferred from usage
options.feedback: Correct: generics defer type selection until call site or inference.

options.text: A fixed type that is locked in when the function is defined
options.feedback: Generics are not fixed; they become concrete when the function is used.

options.text: A runtime template that generates different JavaScript functions per type
options.feedback: Generics do not generate runtime versions; they are compile-time only.

options.text: A syntax that forces all parameters to be of type any
options.feedback: Generics increase precision; they avoid any by preserving relationships.

questions.question: What is the main idea of a generic type in a function signature?

whyOptions.text: They preserve relationships between inputs and outputs without losing specificity
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: the same type parameter ties values together across the signature.

whyOptions.text: They disable compiler errors so code always compiles
whyOptions.feedback: Generics strengthen checking; they do not disable errors.

whyOptions.text: They force all arguments to match one hard-coded global type
whyOptions.feedback: The type is per-call and inferred or provided, not globally hard-coded.

whyOptions.text: They automatically cast values to the expected types at runtime
whyOptions.feedback: TypeScript does not insert runtime casts; generics are erased at runtime.

questions.whyQuestion: Why do generics improve type safety compared to using any?

whyOptions.text: The accumulator becomes immutable so properties cannot be assigned
whyOptions.feedback: Immutability is not implied; the issue is missing type information.

whyOptions.text: The accumulator is automatically widened to never, blocking all assignments
whyOptions.feedback: The issue is not becoming never; it is insufficient structure to type properties.

whyOptions.text: The accumulator forces T to become any, breaking the callback signature

questions.hint: Focus on memory behavior in Linux programs.

options.text: Detecting memory leaks by tracking allocations that are not freed
options.feedback: Correct: it flags memory that was allocated and not returned properly.

options.text: Compiling C/C++ code into optimized binaries with fewer allocations
options.feedback: Valgrind is a runtime analysis tool, not a compiler or optimizer.

options.text: Encrypting program memory to prevent unauthorized reads
options.feedback: Valgrind does not provide encryption; it observes memory usage patterns.

options.text: Automatically freeing memory on behalf of leaking programs
options.feedback: It reports leaks; it does not fix them by freeing memory automatically.

questions.question: What is Valgrind primarily used for, as described in the notes?

whyOptions.text: If memory is allocated and never freed, repeated runs accumulate unreleased memory
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: allocations without corresponding frees indicate leaks across executions.

whyOptions.text: If a variable changes type at runtime, memory is considered leaked
whyOptions.feedback: Type changes are unrelated; leaks concern allocation and deallocation mismatches.

whyOptions.text: If a function returns early, Valgrind assumes all memory is leaked
whyOptions.feedback: Early returns are fine if cleanup occurs; leaks require missing deallocation.

whyOptions.text: If memory is stack-allocated, Valgrind reports it as leaked by default
whyOptions.feedback: Stack memory is managed automatically; leaks typically involve heap allocations.

options.text: Declarations define expected types, while assertions force a type interpretation

options.text: Declarations run at runtime, while assertions only exist in JavaScript output
options.feedback: Neither runs at runtime; both are removed from emitted JavaScript.

options.feedback: Declarations can be explicit; assertions only affect the asserted expression.

options.text: Declarations are unsafe by default, while assertions are always safer
options.feedback: Assertions are riskier because they can hide real type mismatches.

questions.question: Which statement best contrasts type declarations with type assertions in TypeScript?

whyOptions.text: They can mask real mismatches, letting incorrect assumptions compile silently
whyOptions.feedback: Right: assertions shift responsibility to you and can hide type errors.

whyOptions.text: They prevent TypeScript from emitting JavaScript in production builds
whyOptions.feedback: Assertions do not affect emission; they are stripped like other type syntax.

whyOptions.text: They allocate extra memory at runtime for type metadata
whyOptions.feedback: No runtime metadata is allocated because TypeScript types are erased.

whyOptions.text: They force all object properties to become optional, weakening structures

questions.hint: Think about how many repositories hold the whole app.

options.text: A single repository containing multiple parts of an application, like frontend and backend.
options.feedback: Correct: a monorepo centralizes related projects in one shared repository.

options.text: A setup where each service has its own repository and separate versioning.
options.feedback: That describes a multi-repo approach, not a single shared repository.

options.text: A repository that stores only compiled artifacts, not source code.
options.feedback: Monorepos hold source code; artifact repositories serve different purposes.

options.text: A repository that must contain only one package and one deployable unit.
options.feedback: Monorepos typically contain multiple packages or apps, not just one.

questions.question: What best describes a monorepo?

whyOptions.text: It simplifies sharing code and coordinating changes across frontend and backend.
whyOptions.feedback: Right: cross-project refactors and shared tooling are easier in one repo.

whyOptions.text: It guarantees faster runtime performance for deployed services.
whyOptions.feedback: Repo structure affects development workflow, not runtime performance directly.

whyOptions.text: It prevents any dependency version mismatches automatically.
whyOptions.feedback: Versioning can be managed, but mismatches still require tooling and discipline.

whyOptions.text: It eliminates the need for CI pipelines because everything is local.
whyOptions.feedback: CI is still needed for builds, tests, and releases regardless of repo layout.
